summary: The article argues that organizations measuring developer productivity often fail because they rely solely on quantitative metrics like DORA, which don't provide the insights leaders need. The authors advocate for qualitative metrics—measurements derived from human input through surveys—which can capture both subjective attitudes and objective behavioral data. They demonstrate that even DORA's benchmarks are actually collected through qualitative survey methods. The piece concludes that a mixed-methods approach combining qualitative and quantitative data provides the most complete picture of developer productivity, with qualitative data serving as the starting point to identify opportunities before drilling deeper with quantitative metrics.
key_points: Qualitative metrics are measurements comprised of data provided by humans, and can capture both attitudinal (subjective feelings) and behavioral (objective facts) information
Many organizations don't realize DORA's benchmarks for lead time, deployment frequency, and change fail rate are actually captured through qualitative survey methods, not system telemetry
Qualitative metrics can measure intangibles that are otherwise unmeasurable—Google found no combination of 117 proposed metrics could validly indicate technical debt
Survey design requires rigor comparable to software development, treating the response process as an algorithm in the human mind
The recommended approach is to start with qualitative data to identify top opportunities, then use quantitative metrics to drill deeper into specific areas
money_quotes: We cannot rely on this same approach for measuring people.
Engineers aren't robots, they're humans. And just looking at basic numbers doesn't drive the whole story.
If we only look at logs data it doesn't really tell you whether something is good or bad.
Assessment of technical debt relies on the comparison between the current state of a system or codebase versus its imagined ideal state. In other words, human judgment is essential.
Organizations spend a lot on highly qualified humans that can observe and detect problems that log-based metrics can't.
Behavioral data might show you that your release process is fast and efficient. But only attitudinal data could tell you whether it is smooth and painless.
We caution against this phrase because it harmfully and incorrectly implies that data collected from humans is weaker than 'hard metrics' collected from systems.
key_insight: The most effective approach to measuring developer productivity starts with qualitative data from developers themselves to identify opportunities, then uses quantitative metrics to drill deeper—because humans can detect problems that log-based metrics cannot.
